Optimization Measures

Optimization Measures

In the past, IT organizations could address performance issues only at run time, when a performance bottleneck in either the application logic or the physical infrastructure would cause application failure. Performance monitoring and tuning tools have made the issues more manageable by providing early indicators of performance problems, but the best time to address application performance and scalability issues is when you are designing and developing the application. The measures you can implement to optimize performance depend on where you are in the application's lifecycle.

Many optimization measures can have both positive and negative impact, depending on your situation. For example, reusing retrieved data can reduce network traffic, but if the amount of data retrieved is large, the transaction size increases, which can have a negative impact. Performance optimization is always a balancing act.

The following lists for optimizing performance are not exhaustive and the corrective measures do not necessarily apply in all circumstances. In most cases, more detailed information is provided elsewhere in the documentation.

Guidelines

All performance optimization measures implement one or more of the following principles:

  • Enable application partitioning—A multi-tier architecture makes it possible to partition the application over multiple machines and platforms. This makes it easier to deploy the application efficiently, to scale the application to meet increasing demands, and to balance loads as required.
  • Minimize data retrieval and processing—Data retrieval and processing consume the largest part of computing resources and can also affect network traffic. Optimization measures can be implemented during all stages of the application cycle and can affect the data design, the business logic, and the choice and location of components. These all play a role in how data is requested, used, reused, processed, and stored. The goal is to retrieve the optimum amount of data required and process the retrieved data as efficiently as possible.
  • Reduce process contention— Processing one request can interfere with another, as a result of, for example, data locking or queues of synchronous requests waiting for a server.
  • Minimize network traffic—The location of databases, servers, and components affects network traffic. High network traffic can negatively affect performance. Most optimization measures relate to application partitioning and load balancing.
  • Maximize use of available resources—Applications must work within budgetary, computing, and time constraints. Optimization measures such as balancing loads over time and infrastructure, making efficient use of memory, or purchasing additional resources can ensure that these resources are used most effectively within those constraints.

Response Time Expectations

A realistic performance requirement is a prerequisite for successfully optimizing performance. Performance optimization cannot be successful without realistic expectations, even if well-designed.

You can begin setting realistic expectations when you know the computing resources to be used and what their purposes are. This information is normally already available during the inception phase. For example, you should already know the following:

  • Whether the system infrastructure is, for example, a stand-alone PC or a number of medium-sized machines in a wide area network
  • Whether there are only a few occasional users or many users working at various distant locations at the same time

By comparing this information with existing systems, you can estimate the future performance.
